_question: Can the State Bar of California hold public meetings that require attendees to sign in?
_note: The scratch document after Q&A is complete. Three facts were extracted across three sections. One question was generated by the analyst for Section 11131 (asking about Section 11135's protected characteristics), which could not be answered because Section 11135 was not part of the analyzed document. The corresponding request_relevant_section was also filed — the system attempted to retrieve Section 11135 but it was out of scope.

fact_001.content: The State Bar of California is expressly included as a 'state body' for purposes of this open-meeting article (operative April 1, 2016).

fact_002.content: All meetings of a state body must be open and public, and all persons must be permitted to attend any meeting except as otherwise provided in the article.

fact_003.content: A state body may not require any person, as a condition of attending a meeting, to register their name, provide other information, complete a questionnaire, or fulfill any other condition precedent to attendance. However, an attendance list/register/questionnaire may be posted or circulated only if it clearly states participation is voluntary and that all persons may attend regardless of whether they sign/register/complete it. For teleconferenced meetings using an internet website/online platform that requires information to log in, the attendee must be permitted to use a pseudonym or other anonymous information.

question_002.content: What characteristics are 'listed or defined' in Section 11135 (as incorporated by Section 11131's nondiscrimination rule on facility admittance)?

answer_001.content: Section 11131 references 'any characteristic listed or defined in Section 11135' but does not itself list those characteristics; the specifics depend on Section 11135.
